content.plain_english: This week in Year 4 maths, your child is working on Position and direction. The learning focuses on: describe positions on a 2d grid as coordinates in the first quadrant; describe movements between positions as translations of a given unit to the left/right and up/down; and plot specified points and draw sides to complete a given polygon. These skills build directly on what they have already mastered and prepare them for the next steps in the programme of study.
content.in_school: Children handle, sort, and classify 2-D and 3-D shapes, drawing and describing their properties. They use protractors, mirrors, and squared paper to explore angles, symmetry, and position. Explaining what they notice in precise mathematical language is a key part of every lesson.
content.home_activity: Look for real-life examples around the house — a map showing distances, a measuring jug with ml and pints, a food label in grams and kg. Ask your child to read the measurements and convert between them. Road signs in miles are another good context.
dinner_table_questions: Can you show me how to describe positions on a 2d grid as coordinates in the first quadrant?
Was it easy or tricky to describe movements between positions as translations of a given unit to the left/right and up/down? Why?
How would you explain today's maths to someone who has never seen it before?

key_vocabulary.definition: A pair of numbers giving the exact position of a point on a grid — e.g. (3, 4).
The horizontal line on a coordinate grid.
The vertical line on a coordinate grid.
Sliding a shape to a new position without rotating or flipping it.
Flipping a shape over a mirror line.

example_questions: Find a common factor of 24 and 36.
content.times_table_tip: Year 4 children should know all their times tables to 12 × 12 by the end of the year — the national Multiplication Tables Check happens in Year 4. Daily 3-minute practice mixing tables in random order is the best preparation.
